declarations.name: url_github_file
declarations.docComment: Build GitHub file URL for a repository.
declarations.typeSignature: (repo_url: string, file_path: string, line?: number | undefined): string
examples:
```ts
url_github_file('https://github.com/foo/bar', 'src/index.ts')
// => 'https://github.com/foo/bar/blob/main/src/index.ts'
```

```ts
url_github_file('https://github.com/foo/bar', './src/index.ts', 42)
// => 'https://github.com/foo/bar/blob/main/src/index.ts#L42'
```

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: path to the file (leading './' is stripped)

parameters.name: line
parameters.description: optional line number for deep linking

declarations.returnDescription: full GitHub URL to the file on the main branch

declarations.name: repo_url_github_owner
declarations.docComment: Extract GitHub owner/org name from repository URL.
examples:
```ts
repo_url_github_owner('https://github.com/fuzdev/fuz_ui')
// => 'fuzdev'
```

```ts
repo_url_github_owner('https://gitlab.com/foo/bar')
// => null (not a GitHub URL)
```
declarations.returnDescription: owner name, or null if not a valid GitHub URL

declarations.name: url_npm_package
declarations.docComment: Build npm package URL.
examples:
```ts
url_npm_package('@fuzdev/fuz_ui')
// => 'https://www.npmjs.com/package/@fuzdev/fuz_ui'
```
declarations.returnDescription: full npm package page URL

declarations.name: url_logo
declarations.docComment:
Build a library's logo URL from its homepage and optional `logo` path.

Resolves `logo` relative to `homepage_url`, falling back to `favicon.png` at
the homepage root. Returns `null` when there's no homepage.
declarations.typeSignature: (homepage_url: string | null, logo: string | undefined): string | null
examples:
```ts
url_logo('https://fuz.dev', 'logo.svg')
// => 'https://fuz.dev/logo.svg'
```

parameters.name: logo
parameters.description: the `logo` field from `package.json` (relative to the homepage)

declarations.returnDescription: the resolved logo URL, or `null`

declarations.name: package_is_published
declarations.docComment:
Check if a package is published to npm.

A package is considered published if:
- It's not marked as private
- It has exports defined
- Its version is not the initial '0.0.1'
declarations.typeSignature: (package_json: { [x: string]: unknown; name: string; version?: string | undefined; private?: boolean | undefined; description?: string | undefined; tagline?: string | undefined; glyph?: string | undefined; ... 24 more ...; exports?: string | ... 2 more ... | undefined; }): boolean

parameters.name: package_json
parameters.type: { [x: string]: unknown; name: string; version?: string | undefined; private?: boolean | undefined; description?: string | undefined; tagline?: string | undefined; glyph?: string | undefined; logo?: string | undefined; ... 23 more ...; exports?: string | ... 2 more ... | undefined; }

declarations.returnDescription: `true` if the package appears to be published

declarations.name: repo_name_parse
declarations.docComment: Extract repository name without scope from package name.
examples:
```ts
repo_name_parse('@fuzdev/fuz_ui')
// => 'fuz_ui'
```

```ts
repo_name_parse('lodash')
// => 'lodash'
```

throws.description: if scoped package name is malformed

declarations.returnDescription: repository name without scope

declarations.name: repo_url_parse
declarations.docComment:
Parse repository URL from `package.json` format.

Handles both string format and object format with `url` property.
Strips common prefixes ('git+') and suffixes ('.git', '/').
declarations.typeSignature: (repository: string | { [x: string]: unknown; type: string; url: string; directory?: string | undefined; } | undefined): string | null
examples:
```ts
repo_url_parse('https://github.com/foo/bar')
// => 'https://github.com/foo/bar'
```

```ts
repo_url_parse({url: 'git+https://github.com/foo/bar.git'})
// => 'https://github.com/foo/bar'
```

```ts
repo_url_parse(undefined)
// => null
```

parameters.name: repository
parameters.type: string | { [x: string]: unknown; type: string; url: string; directory?: string | undefined; } | undefined

declarations.returnDescription: clean repository URL, or null if not provided

modules.moduleComment:
Package and repository URL helpers.

Generic utilities for building URLs and parsing package/repository metadata.
These functions have no framework dependencies and can be used at build-time or runtime.

URL builders:
- `url_github_file` - GitHub file permalink
- `url_npm_package` - npm package page
- `url_logo` - library logo URL from homepage + `logo`

Parsers:
- `repo_url_parse` - extract repo URL from `package.json` repository field
- `repo_name_parse` - extract repo name from scoped package name
- `repo_url_github_owner` - extract GitHub owner from repo URL

Predicates:
- `package_is_published` - check if package is published to npm

The keys kept when stripping `package.json` down to a `PkgJson` — package
identity plus the Fuz extension fields (`tagline`, `glyph`, `logo`,
`logo_alt`). `exports` and `private` are kept because a consumer derives a
library's `published` status from them; everything omitted stays out of the client.

declarations.docComment: Publish-safe subset of `PackageJson`.

declarations.name: pkg_json_from_package_json
declarations.docComment:
Picks the curated, publish-safe subset from a full `package.json`-shaped
object — the runtime counterpart of the `PkgJson` type, keyed off the same
`pkg_json_keys` so the strip and the type can't drift. Idempotent: an
already-curated `PkgJson` passes through unchanged.

Use it at every boundary that feeds `package.json` into a `LibraryJson` so the
curation the type promises actually holds at runtime — otherwise the full
manifest rides along, typed as the subset (see fuz_ui's `vite_plugin_pkg_json`
for the parallel build-time strip).

`keys` overrides the field set to keep — pass a wider list (typically
`` [...pkg_json_keys, 'keywords'] ``) to expose extra publish-safe fields.
The result is then a superset of `PkgJson`, so the extra fields stay
statically untyped; the same `keys` must reach `library_json_from_modules`
(and the consumer's `virtual:pkg.json` ambient type) for them to survive end
to end.
declarations.typeSignature: (source: { [x: string]: unknown; name: string; version?: string | undefined; private?: boolean | undefined; description?: string | undefined; tagline?: string | undefined; glyph?: string | undefined; ... 24 more ...; exports?: string | ... 2 more ... | undefined; }, keys?: readonly string[]): PkgJson

parameters.name: source
parameters.type: { [x: string]: unknown; name: string; version?: string | undefined; private?: boolean | undefined; description?: string | undefined; tagline?: string | undefined; glyph?: string | undefined; logo?: string | undefined; ... 23 more ...; exports?: string | ... 2 more ... | undefined; }

modules.moduleComment:
The curated, publish-safe subset of `package.json`.

`PkgJson` is the shape served by the `virtual:pkg.json` Vite module (see
fuz_ui's `vite_plugin_pkg_json`) and fed to `LibraryJson.pkg_json`. It
exists to keep the rest of `package.json` — `scripts`, `dependencies`,
`devDependencies`, `engines`, `files`, internal config — out of client
bundles and rendered output. The plugin strips to `pkg_json_keys` at build
time; `PkgJson` is `Pick`ed from the same list, so the runtime strip and the
type can't drift, and the type is strict (accessing a stripped field like
`pkg_json.scripts` is a compile error).

declarations.name: SpawnResultError
declarations.docComment:
Spawn failed before the process could run.

Note: `child` is still present because `node:child_process.spawn` returns a
`ChildProcess` synchronously and then emits `'error'` asynchronously — the
handle exists but the OS process never started.
examples: ENOENT when command not found

Process ran and exited with a code.
`ok` is true when `code` is 0.

declarations.name: SpawnResult

Discriminated union representing all possible spawn outcomes.
Narrow via `result.kind === 'error' | 'exited' | 'signaled'`.

declarations.name: ProcessRegistry
declarations.docComment:
Manages a collection of spawned processes for lifecycle tracking and cleanup.

The default instance `process_registry_default` is used by module-level functions.
Create separate instances for isolated process groups or testing.

members.name: processes
members.docComment: All currently tracked child processes

members.name: spawn
members.docComment:
Spawns a process and tracks it in this registry.
The process is automatically unregistered when it exits.
members.typeSignature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): SpawnedProcess

throws.description: if `timeout_ms` is negative

members.name: spawn_out
members.docComment:
Spawns a process and captures stdout/stderr as strings.
Sets `stdio: 'pipe'` automatically.
members.typeSignature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): Promise<SpawnedOut>

throws.description: if `timeout_ms` is negative

members.returnDescription:
result with captured `stdout` and `stderr`
- `null` means spawn failed (ENOENT, etc.) or stream was unavailable
- `''` (empty string) means process ran but produced no output
- non-empty string contains the captured output

members.name: despawn
members.docComment: Kills a child process and waits for it to exit.

throws.description: if `timeout_ms` is negative

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: kill options including signal and timeout

members.name: despawn_all
members.docComment: Kills all processes in this registry.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: kill options applied to all processes

members.name: attach_error_handler
members.docComment:
Attaches an `uncaughtException` handler that kills all processes before exiting.
Prevents zombie processes when the parent crashes.

By default uses SIGKILL for immediate termination. Set `graceful_timeout_ms`
to attempt SIGTERM first (allowing processes to clean up) before escalating
to SIGKILL after the timeout.

Note: Node's uncaughtException handler cannot await async operations, so
graceful shutdown uses a blocking busy-wait. This may not be sufficient
for processes that need significant cleanup time.
members.typeSignature: (options?: { to_error_label?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; map_error_text?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; handle_error?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => void) | undefined; graceful_timeout_ms?: number | ... 1 more ... | undefined; } | undefined): () => void

throws.description: if a handler is already attached to this registry

parameters.name: options
parameters.type: { to_error_label?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; map_error_text?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; handle_error?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => void) | undefined; graceful_timeout_ms?: number | ... 1 more...

propertyDescriptions.to_error_label: Customize error label, return `null` for default
propertyDescriptions.map_error_text: Customize error text, return `''` to silence
propertyDescriptions.graceful_timeout_ms:
If set, sends SIGTERM first and waits this
many ms before SIGKILL. Recommended: 100-500ms. If null/undefined, uses
immediate SIGKILL (default).

members.returnDescription: cleanup function to remove the handler

Spawns a detached process that continues after parent exits.

Unlike other spawn functions, this is NOT tracked in any `ProcessRegistry`.
The spawned process is meant to outlive the parent (e.g., daemon processes).

declarations.name: RestartableProcess
declarations.docComment:
Handle for a process that can be restarted.
Exposes `closed` promise for observing exits and implementing restart policies.

members.name: restart
members.docComment:
Restart the process, killing the current one if active.
Concurrent calls are coalesced - multiple calls before the first completes
will share the same restart operation.

members.name: kill
members.docComment: Kill the process and set `active` to false

members.name: active
members.docComment:
Whether this handle is managing a process.

Note: This reflects handle state, not whether the underlying OS process is executing.
Remains `true` after a process exits naturally until `kill()` or `restart()` is called.
To check if the process actually exited, await `closed`.

members.name: child
members.docComment: The current child process, or null if not active

members.name: closed
members.docComment: Promise that resolves when the current process exits

members.name: spawned
members.docComment:
Promise that resolves when the initial `spawn_process()` call completes.

Note: This resolves when the spawn syscall returns, NOT when the process
is "ready" or has produced output. For commands that fail immediately
(e.g., ENOENT), `spawned` still resolves - check `closed` for errors.
examples:
```ts
const rp = spawn_restartable_process('node', ['server.js']);
await rp.spawned;  // Safe to access rp.child now
```

declarations.name: spawn_restartable_process
declarations.docComment:
Spawns a process that can be restarted.
Handles concurrent restart calls gracefully.

Note: The `signal` and `timeout_ms` options are reapplied on each restart.
If the AbortSignal is already aborted when `restart()` is called, the new
process will be killed immediately.
declarations.typeSignature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): RestartableProcess

declarations.name: process_is_pid_running
declarations.docComment:
Checks if a process with the given PID is running.
Uses signal 0 which checks existence without sending a signal.

parameters.name: pid
parameters.description: the process ID to check (must be a positive integer)

declarations.returnDescription:
`true` if the process exists (even without permission to signal it),
`false` if the process doesn't exist or if pid is invalid (non-positive, non-integer, NaN, Infinity)

declarations.name: random_float
declarations.docComment: Generates a random `number` between `min` and `max`.
declarations.typeSignature: (min: number, max: number, random?: () => number): number

declarations.name: random_int
declarations.docComment:
Returns a random integer between `min` and `max` inclusive.
Node's `randomInt` is similar but exclusive of the max value, and makes `min` optional -
https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v20.x/api/crypto.html#cryptorandomintmin-max-callback
declarations.typeSignature: (min: number, max: number, random?: () => number): number

declarations.name: random_boolean
declarations.docComment: Generates a random `boolean`.

declarations.name: random_item
declarations.docComment: Selects a random item from an array.
declarations.typeSignature: <T extends ReadonlyArray<any>>(arr: T, random?: () => number): ArrayElement<T>

declarations.name: shuffle
declarations.docComment: Mutates `array` with random ordering.
declarations.typeSignature: <T extends Array<any>>(array: T, random?: ((min: number, max: number, random?: () => number) => number) | undefined): T

mutates.array: randomly reorders elements in place using Fisher-Yates shuffle

parameters.name: random
parameters.type: ((min: number, max: number, random?: () => number) => number) | undefined

modules.moduleComment:
Random helpers that accept an optional `random` parameter,
defaulting to `Math.random`. Pass a seeded PRNG for reproducible results:

- `create_random_xoshiro` — fast, high-quality numeric seeding (recommended)
- `create_random_alea` — supports string and variadic seeds

declarations.name: create_random_alea
declarations.docComment:
Seeded pseudo-random number generator.
DO NOT USE when security matters, use webcrypto APIs instead.

modules.moduleComment:
Alea: a seedable pseudo-random number generator by Johannes Baagøe.
Supports variadic and string seeds (`create_random_alea('my', 3, 'seeds')`).
For numeric seeds, prefer `create_random_xoshiro` which is faster with equal quality.

DO NOT USE when security matters — use the Web Crypto API (`crypto.getRandomValues`) instead.

Alea passes all 11 distribution quality tests at 10M samples,
performing on par with `Math.random` (V8's xorshift128+):

- mean, variance, chi-squared uniformity, Kolmogorov-Smirnov
- lag-1 through lag-8 autocorrelation
- runs test, gap test, permutation test (triples)
- bit-level frequency (bits 0-7)
- 2D serial pairs (25x25 through 200x200 grids)
- birthday spacings (Marsaglia parameters)

Speed is ~19% slower than `Math.random` (~12.2M ops/sec vs ~15.0M ops/sec).

To reproduce:

```bash
npm run benchmark_random_quality          # distribution tests (N=1M)
npm run benchmark_random_quality -- --deep # thorough (N=10M, multi-trial)
npm run benchmark_random                  # speed comparison
```

@see https://github.com/nquinlan/better-random-numbers-for-javascript-mirror

declarations.name: create_random_xoshiro
declarations.docComment:
Seeded pseudo-random number generator using the Xoshiro128** algorithm.
DO NOT USE when security matters, use webcrypto APIs instead.

modules.moduleComment:
Xoshiro128**: a seedable pseudo-random number generator by Blackman & Vigna (2018).
Recommended for reproducible randomness (testing, simulations, procedural generation).

DO NOT USE when security matters — use the Web Crypto API (`crypto.getRandomValues`) instead.

Xoshiro128** has a 2^128-1 period and passes BigCrush.
It uses pure 32-bit arithmetic (shifts, rotates, XOR, multiply)
making it very fast in JavaScript via `Math.imul`.

Xoshiro128** passes all 11 distribution quality tests at 10M samples,
performing on par with `Math.random` (V8's xorshift128+):

- mean, variance, chi-squared uniformity, Kolmogorov-Smirnov
- lag-1 through lag-8 autocorrelation
- runs test, gap test, permutation test (triples)
- bit-level frequency (bits 0-7)
- 2D serial pairs (25x25 through 200x200 grids)
- birthday spacings (Marsaglia parameters)

Speed is near-native (~4% slower than `Math.random`) and ~18% faster than Alea
(~14.4M ops/sec vs ~15.0M and ~12.2M respectively).

To reproduce:

```bash
npm run benchmark_random_quality          # distribution tests (N=1M)
npm run benchmark_random_quality -- --deep # thorough (N=10M, multi-trial)
npm run benchmark_random                  # speed comparison
```

@see https://prng.di.unimi.it/
@see https://vigna.di.unimi.it/ftp/papers/ScrambledLinear.pdf

declarations.name: escape_regexp
declarations.docComment: Escapes a string for literal matching in a RegExp.

declarations.name: reset_regexp
declarations.docComment:
Reset a RegExp's lastIndex to 0 for global and sticky patterns.
Ensures consistent behavior by clearing state that affects subsequent matches.

mutates.regexp: sets lastIndex to 0 if regexp is global or sticky

declarations.name: Result
declarations.docComment:
An alternative pattern to throwing and catching errors.
Uses the type system to strongly type error return values when desired.
Catching errors is then reserved for unexpected situations.

declarations.name: OK
declarations.docComment: Frozen object representing a successful result.

declarations.name: NOT_OK
declarations.docComment: Frozen object representing a failed result.

declarations.name: unwrap
declarations.docComment:
A helper that says,
"hey I know this is wrapped in a `Result`, but I expect it to be `ok`,
so if it's not, I understand it will throw an error that wraps the result".
declarations.typeSignature: <TValue extends { value?: unknown; }, TError extends { message?: string; }>(result: Result<TValue, TError>, message?: string | undefined): TValue["value"]

throws.description: if `result.ok` is false

declarations.name: ResultError
declarations.docComment:
A custom error class that's thrown by `unwrap`.
Wraps a failed `Result` with an optional message,
and also accepts an optional message that overrides the result's.
Useful for generic handling of unwrapped results
to forward custom messages and other failed result data.

members.name: constructor
members.typeSignature: (result: { ok: false; message?: string | undefined; }, message?: string | undefined, options?: ErrorOptions | undefined): ResultError

declarations.name: unwrap_error
declarations.docComment:
A helper that does the opposite of `unwrap`, throwing if the `Result` is ok.
Note that while `unwrap` returns the wrapped `value`, `unwrap_error` returns the entire result.
declarations.typeSignature: <TError extends object>(result: Result<object, TError>, message?: string): { ok: false; } & TError

throws.description: if `result.ok` is true

declarations.name: Sortable
declarations.docComment: Minimum shape required for topological sorting.

declarations.name: TopologicalSortResult
declarations.docComment: Result of topological sort.

declarations.name: topological_sort
declarations.docComment:
Sort items by their dependencies using Kahn's algorithm.

Returns items ordered so that dependencies come before dependents.
If a cycle is detected, returns an error with the cycle path.
declarations.typeSignature: <T extends Sortable>(items: T[], label?: string): TopologicalSortResult<T>
declarations.returnDescription: sorted items or error if cycle detected

modules.moduleComment:
Generic topological sort using Kahn's algorithm.

Orders items so that dependencies come before dependents.
Works with any item type that has `id` and optional `depends_on`.

declarations.name: stats_mean
declarations.docComment: Calculate the mean (average) of an array of numbers.

declarations.name: stats_median
declarations.docComment:
Calculate the median of an array of numbers.
NaN values are filtered out before computing.

declarations.name: stats_std_dev
declarations.docComment:
Calculate the standard deviation of an array of numbers.
Uses population standard deviation (divides by n, not n-1).
For benchmarks with many samples, this is typically appropriate.

declarations.name: stats_variance
declarations.docComment: Calculate the variance of an array of numbers.

declarations.name: stats_percentile
declarations.docComment:
Calculate a percentile of an array of numbers using linear interpolation.
Uses the "R-7" method (default in R, NumPy, Excel) which interpolates between
data points for more accurate percentile estimates, especially with smaller samples.

declarations.name: stats_cv
declarations.docComment:
Calculate the coefficient of variation (CV).
CV = standard deviation / mean, expressed as a ratio.
Useful for comparing relative variability between datasets.

declarations.name: stats_min_max
declarations.docComment:
Calculate min and max values.
NaN values are ignored.

declarations.name: StatsOutlierResult
declarations.docComment: Result from outlier detection.

members.name: cleaned
members.docComment: Values after removing outliers

members.name: outliers
members.docComment: Detected outlier values

declarations.name: StatsOutliersIqrOptions
declarations.docComment: Configuration options for IQR outlier detection.

members.name: iqr_multiplier
members.docComment: Multiplier for IQR bounds (default: 1.5)

members.name: min_sample_size
members.docComment: Minimum sample size to perform outlier detection (default: 3)

declarations.name: stats_outliers_iqr
declarations.docComment:
Detect outliers using the IQR (Interquartile Range) method.
Values outside [Q1 - multiplier*IQR, Q3 + multiplier*IQR] are considered outliers.

declarations.name: StatsOutliersMadOptions
declarations.docComment: Configuration options for MAD outlier detection.

members.name: z_score_threshold
members.docComment: Modified Z-score threshold for outlier detection (default: 3.5)

members.name: z_score_extreme
members.docComment: Extreme Z-score threshold when too many outliers detected (default: 5.0)

members.name: mad_constant
members.docComment: MAD constant for normal distribution (default: 0.6745)

members.name: outlier_ratio_high
members.docComment: Ratio threshold to switch to extreme mode (default: 0.3)

members.name: outlier_ratio_extreme
members.docComment: Ratio threshold to switch to keep-closest mode (default: 0.4)

members.name: outlier_keep_ratio
members.docComment: Ratio of values to keep in keep-closest mode (default: 0.8)

members.name: min_sample_size
members.docComment: Minimum sample size to perform outlier detection (default: 3)

members.name: iqr_options
members.docComment: Options to pass to IQR fallback when MAD is zero

declarations.name: stats_outliers_mad
declarations.docComment:
Detect outliers using the MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) method.
More robust than IQR for skewed distributions.
Uses modified Z-score: |0.6745 * (x - median) / MAD|
Values with modified Z-score > threshold are considered outliers.

declarations.name: STATS_CONFIDENCE_Z_SCORES
declarations.docComment: Common z-scores for confidence intervals.

declarations.name: stats_confidence_level_to_z_score
declarations.docComment:
Convert a confidence level (0-1) to a z-score.
Uses a lookup table for common values, approximates others.
examples:
```ts
stats_confidence_level_to_z_score(0.95); // 1.96
stats_confidence_level_to_z_score(0.99); // 2.576
```

throws.description: if `level` is not in the open interval (0, 1)

declarations.name: StatsConfidenceIntervalOptions
declarations.docComment: Configuration options for confidence interval calculation.

members.name: z_score
members.docComment: Z-score for confidence level (default: 1.96 for 95% CI)

members.name: confidence_level
members.docComment: Confidence level (0-1), alternative to z_score. If both provided, z_score takes precedence.

declarations.name: stats_confidence_interval
declarations.docComment: Calculate confidence interval for the mean.
declarations.returnDescription: `[lower_bound, upper_bound]`

declarations.name: stats_confidence_interval_from_summary
declarations.docComment:
Calculate confidence interval from summary statistics (mean, std_dev, sample_size).
Useful when raw data is not available.
declarations.typeSignature: (mean: number, std_dev: number, sample_size: number, options?: StatsConfidenceIntervalOptions | undefined): [number, number]
declarations.returnDescription: `[lower_bound, upper_bound]`

declarations.name: StatsWelchTTestResult
declarations.docComment: Result from Welch's t-test calculation.

members.name: t_statistic
members.docComment: The t-statistic

members.name: degrees_of_freedom
members.docComment: Welch-Satterthwaite degrees of freedom

declarations.name: stats_welch_t_test
declarations.docComment:
Calculate Welch's t-test statistic and degrees of freedom.
Welch's t-test is more robust than Student's t-test when variances are unequal.

Params suffixed `1` describe the first sample, `2` the second.
declarations.typeSignature: (mean1: number, std1: number, n1: number, mean2: number, std2: number, n2: number): StatsWelchTTestResult

declarations.name: stats_normal_cdf
declarations.docComment: Standard normal CDF approximation (Abramowitz and Stegun formula 7.1.26).

declarations.name: stats_ln_gamma
declarations.docComment: Log gamma function approximation (Lanczos approximation).

declarations.name: stats_incomplete_beta
declarations.docComment:
Approximate regularized incomplete beta function for p-value calculation.
Uses continued fraction expansion for reasonable accuracy.
declarations.typeSignature: (x: number, a: number, b: number): number

declarations.name: stats_t_distribution_p_value
declarations.docComment:
Approximate two-tailed p-value from t-distribution.
For large df (>100), uses normal approximation.
For smaller df, uses incomplete beta function.
declarations.returnDescription: two-tailed p-value

modules.moduleComment:
Statistical analysis utilities.
Pure functions with zero dependencies - can be used standalone for any data analysis.

declarations.name: truncate
declarations.docComment: Truncates a string to a maximum length, adding a suffix if needed that defaults to `...`.
declarations.typeSignature: (str: string, maxLength: number, suffix?: string): string

declarations.name: strip_start
declarations.docComment: Removes characters inclusive of `stripped`.

declarations.name: strip_end
declarations.docComment: Removes characters inclusive of `stripped`.

declarations.name: strip_after
declarations.docComment: Removes characters inclusive of `stripped`.

declarations.name: strip_before
declarations.docComment: Removes characters inclusive of `stripped`.

declarations.name: ensure_start
declarations.docComment: Adds the substring `ensured` to the start of the `source` string if it's not already present.

declarations.name: ensure_end
declarations.docComment: Adds the substring `ensured` to the end of the `source` string if it's not already present.

declarations.name: deindent
declarations.docComment: Removes leading and trailing spaces from each line of a string.

declarations.name: plural
declarations.docComment: Returns a plural suffix based on a count.
declarations.typeSignature: (count: number | null | undefined, suffix?: string): string

declarations.name: count_graphemes
declarations.docComment: Returns the count of graphemes in a string, the individually rendered characters.

declarations.name: strip_ansi
declarations.docComment: Strips ANSI escape sequences from a string.

declarations.name: stringify
declarations.docComment: Stringifies a value like `JSON.stringify` but with some corner cases handled better.

declarations.name: string_display_width
declarations.docComment:
Calculate the display width of a string in terminal columns.
- Strips ANSI escape codes (they have 0 width)
- Emojis and other wide characters take 2 columns
- Tab characters take 4 columns
- Newlines and other control characters take 0 columns
- Uses `Intl.Segmenter` to properly handle grapheme clusters (e.g., family emoji "👨‍👩‍👧‍👦")

declarations.name: pad_width
declarations.docComment: Pad a string to a target display width (accounting for wide characters).

declarations.name: levenshtein_distance
declarations.docComment:
Calculates the Levenshtein distance between two strings.
Useful for typo detection and fuzzy matching.
declarations.returnDescription: the edit distance between the strings

declarations.name: escape_js_string
declarations.docComment:
Escapes a string for use inside a single-quoted JS string literal.

Uses a single-pass regex replacement to escape backslashes, single quotes,
newlines, carriage returns, and Unicode line/paragraph separators.

declarations.name: string_is_binary
declarations.docComment:
Check if content appears to be binary.

Checks for null bytes in the first 8KB of content.

declarations.name: PreprocessImportInfo
declarations.docComment: Import metadata for a single import specifier.

members.name: path
members.docComment: The module path to import from.

members.name: kind
members.docComment: Whether this is a default or named import.

declarations.name: ResolvedComponentImport
declarations.docComment: Information about a resolved component import.

members.name: import_node
members.docComment: The `ImportDeclaration` AST node that provides this name.

members.name: specifier
members.docComment: The specific import specifier for this name.

declarations.name: find_attribute
declarations.docComment:
Finds an attribute by name on a component AST node.

Iterates the node's `attributes` array and returns the first `Attribute`
node whose `name` matches. Skips `SpreadAttribute`, directive, and other node types.
declarations.returnDescription: the matching `Attribute` node, or `undefined` if not found

declarations.name: evaluate_static_expr
declarations.docComment:
Recursively evaluates an expression AST node to a static string value.

Handles string `Literal`, `TemplateLiteral` (including interpolations when all
expressions resolve), `BinaryExpression` with `+`, and `Identifier` lookup
via an optional bindings map built by `build_static_bindings`.
Returns `null` for dynamic expressions, non-string literals, or unsupported node types.
declarations.typeSignature: (expr: Expression, bindings?: ReadonlyMap<string, string> | undefined): string | null

parameters.name: bindings
parameters.description: optional map of variable names to their resolved static string values

declarations.returnDescription: the resolved static string, or `null` if the expression is dynamic

declarations.name: extract_static_string
declarations.docComment:
Extracts a static string value from a Svelte attribute value AST node.

Handles three forms:
- Boolean `true` (bare attribute like `inline`) -- returns `null`.
- Array with a single `Text` node (quoted attribute like `content="text"`) --
  returns the text data.
- `ExpressionTag` (expression like `content={'text'}`) -- delegates to `evaluate_static_expr`.

Returns `null` for null literals, mixed arrays, dynamic expressions, and non-string values.
declarations.typeSignature: (value: true | ExpressionTag | (ExpressionTag | Text)[], bindings?: ReadonlyMap<string, string> | undefined): string | null

parameters.name: bindings
parameters.description: optional map of variable names to their resolved static string values

declarations.returnDescription: the resolved static string, or `null` if the value is dynamic

declarations.name: ConditionalChainBranch
declarations.docComment: A single branch in a conditional chain extracted from nested ternary expressions.

members.name: test_source
members.docComment: The source text of the test expression, or `null` for the final else branch.

members.name: value
members.docComment: The resolved static string value for this branch.

declarations.name: try_extract_conditional_chain
declarations.docComment:
Extracts a chain of conditional expressions where all leaf values are static strings.

Handles nested ternaries like `a ? 'x' : b ? 'y' : 'z'` by iteratively walking
the right-recursive `ConditionalExpression` chain. At each level, evaluates the
consequent via `evaluate_static_expr` and continues into the alternate if it is
another `ConditionalExpression`. The final alternate is the else branch.

Returns `null` if the attribute value is not an `ExpressionTag` containing a
`ConditionalExpression`, if any leaf fails to resolve to a static string, or
if the chain exceeds 10 branches (safety limit).

A 2-branch result covers the simple ternary case (`a ? 'x' : 'y'`).
declarations.typeSignature: (value: true | ExpressionTag | (ExpressionTag | Text)[], source: string, bindings: ReadonlyMap<string, string>): ConditionalChainBranch[] | null

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: the full source string (needed to slice test expression source text)

parameters.name: bindings
parameters.description: map of variable names to their resolved static string values

declarations.returnDescription: array of conditional chain branches, or `null` if not extractable

declarations.name: build_static_bindings
declarations.docComment:
Builds a map of statically resolvable `const` bindings from a Svelte AST.

Scans top-level `const` variable declarations in both instance and module scripts.
For each declarator with a plain `Identifier` pattern and a statically evaluable
initializer, adds the binding to the map. Processes declarations in source order
so that chained references resolve: `const a = 'x'; const b = a;` maps `b` to `'x'`.

Skips destructuring patterns, `let`/`var` declarations, and declarations
whose initializers reference dynamic values.

parameters.name: ast
parameters.description: the parsed Svelte AST root node

declarations.returnDescription: map of variable names to their resolved static string values

declarations.name: resolve_component_names
declarations.docComment:
Resolves local names that import from specified source paths.

Scans `ImportDeclaration` nodes in both the instance and module scripts.
Handles default, named, and aliased imports. Skips namespace imports
and `import type` declarations (both whole-declaration and per-specifier).
Returns import node references alongside names to support import removal.

parameters.name: ast
parameters.description: the parsed Svelte AST root node

parameters.name: component_imports
parameters.description: array of import source paths to match against

declarations.returnDescription: map of local names to their resolved import info

declarations.name: find_import_insert_position
declarations.docComment:
Finds the position to insert new import statements within a script block.

Returns the end position of the last `ImportDeclaration`, or the start
of the script body content if no imports exist.

declarations.name: generate_import_lines
declarations.docComment:
Generates indented import statement lines from an import map.

Default imports produce `import Name from 'path';` lines.
Named imports are grouped by path into `import {a, b} from 'path';` lines.

parameters.name: imports
parameters.description: map of local names to their import info

declarations.returnDescription: a string of newline-separated import statements

declarations.name: has_identifier_in_tree
declarations.docComment:
Checks if an identifier with the given name appears anywhere in an AST subtree.

Recursively walks all object and array properties of the tree, matching
ESTree `Identifier` nodes (`{type: 'Identifier', name}`). Nodes in the
`skip` set are excluded from traversal — used to skip `ImportDeclaration`
nodes so the import's own specifier identifier doesn't false-positive.

Skips `Identifier` nodes in non-reference positions defined by
`NON_REFERENCE_FIELDS` — for example, `obj.Mdz` (non-computed member property),
`{ Mdz: value }` (non-computed object key), and statement labels.

Safe for Svelte template ASTs: `Component.name` is a plain string property
(not an `Identifier` node), so `<Mdz>` tags do not produce false matches.
declarations.typeSignature: (node: unknown, name: string, skip?: Set<unknown> | undefined): boolean

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: the identifier name to look for

parameters.name: skip
parameters.description: set of AST nodes to skip during traversal

declarations.returnDescription: `true` if a matching `Identifier` node is found

declarations.name: escape_svelte_text
declarations.docComment:
Escapes text for safe embedding in Svelte template markup.

Uses a single-pass regex replacement to avoid corruption that occurs with sequential
`.replace()` calls (where the second replace matches characters introduced by the first).

Escapes four characters:
- `{` → `{'{'}` and `}` → `{'}'}` — prevents Svelte expression interpretation
- `<` → `<` — prevents HTML/Svelte tag interpretation
- `&` → `&` — prevents HTML entity interpretation

The `&` escaping is necessary because runtime `MdzNodeView.svelte` renders text
with `{node.content}` (a Svelte expression), which auto-escapes `&` to `&`.
The preprocessor emits raw template text where `&` is NOT auto-escaped, so
manual escaping is required to match the runtime behavior.

declarations.name: remove_variable_declaration
declarations.docComment:
Removes a single-declarator `VariableDeclaration` from source using MagicString.

Consumes leading whitespace (tabs/spaces) and trailing newline to avoid leaving
blank lines. Only safe for single-declarator statements (`const x = 'val';`);
callers must verify `node.declarations.length === 1` before calling.
declarations.typeSignature: (s: { remove: (start: number, end: number) => unknown; }, declaration_node: VariableDeclaration & { start: number; end: number; }, source: string): void

parameters.name: declaration_node
parameters.description: the `VariableDeclaration` AST node with Svelte position data

declarations.name: remove_import_declaration
declarations.docComment:
Removes an `ImportDeclaration` from source using MagicString.

Consumes leading whitespace (tabs/spaces) and trailing newline to avoid leaving
blank lines.
declarations.typeSignature: (s: { remove: (start: number, end: number) => unknown; }, import_node: ImportDeclaration & { start: number; end: number; }, source: string): void

parameters.name: import_node
parameters.description: the `ImportDeclaration` AST node with Svelte position data

declarations.name: remove_import_specifier
declarations.docComment:
Removes a specifier from a multi-specifier import declaration by
reconstructing the statement without the removed specifier.

Overwrites the entire declaration range to avoid character-level comma surgery.

Handles:
- `import Mdz, {other} from '...'` → `import {other} from '...'`
- `import {default as Mdz, other} from '...'` → `import {other} from '...'`
- `import {Mdz, other} from '...'` → `import {other} from '...'`
declarations.typeSignature: (s: { overwrite: (start: number, end: number, content: string) => unknown; }, node: ImportDeclaration & { start: number; end: number; }, specifier_to_remove: ImportSpecifier | ImportDefaultSpecifier | ImportNamespaceSpecifier, source: string, additional_lines?: string): void

mutates.s: overwrites the import declaration's character range

parameters.name: s
parameters.type: { overwrite: (start: number, end: number, content: string) => unknown; }

parameters.name: additional_lines
parameters.description:
extra content appended after the reconstructed import
(used to bundle new imports into the overwrite to avoid MagicString boundary conflicts).

declarations.name: handle_preprocess_error
declarations.docComment: Handles errors during Svelte preprocessing with configurable behavior.
declarations.typeSignature: (error: unknown, prefix: string, filename: string | undefined, on_error: "log" | "throw"): void

throws.description: wrapping `error` (with original as `cause`) when `on_error` is `'throw'`

parameters.name: on_error
parameters.description: `'throw'` to re-throw as a new Error, `'log'` to console.error

modules.moduleComment:
Shared helper functions for Svelte preprocessors.

Provides AST utilities for detecting static content, resolving imports,
managing import statements, and escaping strings for Svelte templates.
Used by `svelte_preprocess_mdz` in fuz_ui, `svelte_preprocess_fuz_code`
in fuz_code, and potentially other Svelte preprocessors.

Uses `import type` from `svelte/compiler` for AST types only — no runtime
Svelte dependency. Consumers must have `svelte` installed for type resolution.

declarations.name: assert_property
declarations.docComment:
Narrows a discriminated union by a literal property value, failing the test
if the value doesn't match. The assertion signature propagates the narrowed
type so callers can access variant-specific fields directly.

Works with any discriminator key (`kind`, `ok`, `type`, `_tag`, etc.).
declarations.typeSignature: <R extends object, P extends keyof R, const V extends R[P]>(obj: R, property: P, value: V): asserts obj is Extract<R, Record<P, V>>

declarations.name: assert_rejects
declarations.docComment:
Asserts that `fn` rejects with an `Error`.
Optionally matches the error message against `pattern`.
Returns the caught `Error` for further assertions by the caller.

`assert.fail` is placed after the catch block so that assertion failures
from the test itself are not swallowed by the catch.

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: optional regex to match against the error message

declarations.returnDescription: the caught `Error`

declarations.name: MockLogger
declarations.docComment:
A mock `Logger` with `vi.fn()` methods and call tracking arrays.
Assignable to `Logger` for use in code under test.
Each tracking array captures the first argument of each call.
For full call details, use `vi.fn()` introspection on the methods directly.

members.name: #cached_st
members.typeSignature: (format: ForegroundColors | BackgroundColors | Modifiers | (ForegroundColors | BackgroundColors | Modifiers)[], text: string, options?: StyleTextOptions | undefined) => string

members.name: level
members.docComment: Dynamic getter for level - checks override, then parent, then default.

members.name: colors
members.docComment:
Dynamic getter for colors - checks override, then parent, then environment variables.

Colors are disabled if either the `NO_COLOR` or `CLAUDECODE` environment variable is set.
The `CLAUDECODE` check disables colors in Claude Code environments where ANSI color codes
may not render correctly in the output.

members.name: console
members.docComment: Dynamic getter for console - checks override, then parent, then global console.

members.name: root
members.docComment:
Gets the root logger by walking up the parent chain.
Useful for setting global configuration that affects all child loggers.

members.name: clear_level_override
members.docComment:
Clears the level override for this logger, restoring inheritance from parent.
After calling this, the logger will dynamically inherit the level from its parent
(or use the default level if it has no parent).

mutates.this: clears `#level_override` and the cached `#cached_level_string` / `#cached_level` so subsequent calls walk the parent chain

members.name: clear_colors_override
members.docComment:
Clears the colors override for this logger, restoring inheritance from parent.
After calling this, the logger will dynamically inherit colors from its parent
(or use the default colors behavior if it has no parent).

mutates.this: clears `#colors_override` and invalidates the cached prefix strings (`#cached_colors`, `#cached_error`, `#cached_warn`, `#cached_info`, `#cached_debug`) so prefixes recompute on next log call

members.name: clear_console_override
members.docComment:
Clears the console override for this logger, restoring inheritance from parent.
After calling this, the logger will dynamically inherit the console from its parent
(or use the global console if it has no parent).

members.name: #ensure_cache_valid
members.docComment:
Ensures prefix cache is valid by checking if colors configuration changed.
Uses pull-based invalidation: checks colors on each access and invalidates cached
prefixes if colors changed. This automatically handles inheritance changes since
`this.colors` getter walks the parent chain on each access.

Invalidates all 4 cached prefix strings when colors change, since they all depend
on the color configuration.

members.name: #format_label
members.docComment:
Formats the label portion of log output with given styleText function.
Applies color styling if enabled, otherwise returns plain bracketed label.
members.typeSignature: (st: (format: ForegroundColors | BackgroundColors | Modifiers | (ForegroundColors | BackgroundColors | Modifiers)[], text: string, options?: StyleTextOptions | undefined) => string, colored: boolean): string

parameters.name: st
parameters.type: (format: ForegroundColors | BackgroundColors | Modifiers | (ForegroundColors | BackgroundColors | Modifiers)[], text: string, options?: StyleTextOptions | undefined) => string

members.name: #get_error_prefix
members.docComment:
Gets the formatted error prefix, lazily computing and caching if needed.
Lazy computation means prefixes are only built when the corresponding log method
is first called, avoiding work for unused log levels.

members.name: #get_warn_prefix
members.docComment: Gets the formatted warn prefix, lazily computing and caching if needed.

members.name: #get_info_prefix
members.docComment:
Gets the formatted info prefix, lazily computing and caching if needed.
Note: info has no colored prefix character, only the label.

members.name: #get_debug_prefix
members.docComment: Gets the formatted debug prefix, lazily computing and caching if needed.

members.name: #get_cached_level
members.docComment:
Gets the cached numeric level value, updating cache if level changed.
Called on every log method invocation to check if the message should be filtered.
Caches the numeric value (from LOG_LEVEL_VALUES dictionary) to avoid repeated lookups.
Uses pull-based invalidation: checks `this.level` getter which handles inheritance.

members.name: child
members.docComment:
Creates a child logger with automatic label concatenation.
Children inherit parent configuration unless overridden.
examples:
```ts
const app_log = new Logger('app');
const db_log = app_log.child('db'); // label: 'app:db'
const query_log = db_log.child('query'); // label: 'app:db:query'
```

throws.description: if label is an empty string

parameters.name: label
parameters.description:
child label (will be concatenated with parent label using `:`)
Cannot be an empty string - empty labels would result in confusing output like `parent:`
with a trailing colon. Use `undefined` or `''` only for root loggers.

members.returnDescription: new `Logger` instance with concatenated label

members.name: error
members.docComment:
Logs an error message with `🞩error🞩` prefix.
Only outputs if current level is `error` or higher.

members.name: warn
members.docComment:
Logs a warning message with `⚑warn⚑` prefix.
Only outputs if current level is `warn` or higher.

members.name: info
members.docComment:
Logs an informational message.
Unlike error/warn/debug, info has no character prefix - only the label is shown.
This keeps standard output clean since info is the default log level.
Only outputs if current level is `info` or higher.

members.name: debug
members.docComment:
Logs a debug message with `┆debug┆` prefix.
Only outputs if current level is `debug`.

members.name: raw
members.docComment:
Logs raw output without any prefix, formatting, or level filtering.
Bypasses the logger's level checking, prefix formatting, and color application entirely.

Note: This method ignores the configured log level - it always outputs regardless of
whether the logger is set to `'off'` or any other level.

declarations.name: create_mock_logger
declarations.docComment:
Creates a mock `Logger` with `vi.fn()` on each logging method
and tracking arrays for inspecting logged messages.
Follows the fuz-stack convention of plain object mocks over mocking libraries.

modules.moduleComment:
Shared test assertions for the `@fuzdev` ecosystem.

Extends the fuz-stack testing conventions (`assert` from vitest, tests in `src/test/`,
plain object mocks) with reusable helpers for patterns that appear across multiple repos.
Only depends on vitest — safe for fuz_util's zero-runtime-deps constraint.

members.name: delay
members.docComment: Enforced milliseconds between calls. For `when='trailing'` this is the debounce delay.

members.name: when
members.docComment: When to call the throttled function. Defaults to `both`.

declarations.name: throttle
declarations.docComment:
Throttles calls to a callback that returns a void promise.
Immediately invokes the callback on the first call unless `leading=false`.
If the throttled function is called while the promise is already pending,
the call is queued to run after the pending promise completes plus `delay`,
and only the last call is invoked.
In other words, all calls and their args are discarded
during the pending window except for the most recent.
Unlike debouncing, this calls the throttled callback
both on the leading and trailing edges of the delay window by default,
and this can be customized by setting `when` to `'leading'` or `'trailing'`.
It also differs from a queue where every call to the throttled callback eventually runs.
declarations.typeSignature: <T extends (...args: Array<any>) => Promise<void>>(cb: T, { delay, when }?: ThrottleOptions): T
declarations.returnDescription: same as `cb`

declarations.name: Timer
declarations.docComment:
Timer interface for measuring elapsed time.
Returns time in nanoseconds for maximum precision.

members.name: now
members.docComment: Get current time in nanoseconds

declarations.name: timer_node
declarations.docComment:
Node.js high-resolution timer using process.hrtime.bigint().
Provides true nanosecond precision.

declarations.name: timer_browser
declarations.docComment:
Browser high-resolution timer using performance.now().
Converts milliseconds to nanoseconds for consistent API.

**Precision varies by browser due to Spectre/Meltdown mitigations:**
- Chrome: ~100μs (coarsened)
- Firefox: ~1ms (rounded)
- Safari: ~100μs
- Node.js: ~1μs

For nanosecond-precision benchmarks, use Node.js with `timer_node`.

declarations.name: timer_default
declarations.docComment:
Auto-detected timer based on environment.
Uses process.hrtime in Node.js, performance.now() in browsers.
The timer function is detected once and cached for performance.

declarations.name: TIME_NS_PER_US
declarations.docComment: Time units and conversions.

declarations.name: time_ns_to_us
declarations.docComment: Convert nanoseconds to microseconds.

declarations.name: time_ns_to_ms
declarations.docComment: Convert nanoseconds to milliseconds.

declarations.name: time_ns_to_sec
declarations.docComment: Convert nanoseconds to seconds.

declarations.name: TimeUnit
declarations.docComment: Time unit for formatting.

declarations.name: TIME_UNIT_DISPLAY
declarations.docComment: Display labels for time units (uses proper Unicode μ for microseconds).

declarations.name: time_unit_detect_best
declarations.docComment:
Detect the best time unit for a set of nanosecond values.
Chooses the unit where most values fall in the range 1-9999.

declarations.name: time_format
declarations.docComment: Format time with a specific unit.
declarations.typeSignature: (ns: number, unit: TimeUnit, decimals?: number): string
declarations.returnDescription: formatted string like "3.87μs"

declarations.name: time_format_adaptive
declarations.docComment: Format time with adaptive units (ns/μs/ms/s) based on magnitude.
examples:
```ts
time_format_adaptive(1500) // "1.50μs"
time_format_adaptive(3870) // "3.87μs"
time_format_adaptive(1500000) // "1.50ms"
time_format_adaptive(1500000000) // "1.50s"
```
declarations.returnDescription: formatted string like "3.87μs" or "1.23ms"

declarations.name: TimeResult
declarations.docComment:
Result from timing a function execution.
All times in nanoseconds for maximum precision.

members.docComment: Elapsed time in nanoseconds

members.name: elapsed_us
members.docComment: Elapsed time in microseconds (convenience)

members.name: elapsed_ms
members.docComment: Elapsed time in milliseconds (convenience)

members.name: started_at_ns
members.docComment: Start time in nanoseconds (from timer.now())

members.name: ended_at_ns
members.docComment: End time in nanoseconds (from timer.now())

declarations.docComment: Time a synchronous function execution.

declarations.name: Flavored
declarations.docComment:
The `Flavored` and `Branded` type helpers add varying degrees of nominal typing to other types.
This is especially useful with primitives like strings and numbers.
seeAlso: https://spin.atomicobject.com/typescript-flexible-nominal-typing/ * `Flavored` is a looser form of `Branded` that trades explicitness and a little safety in some cases for ergonomics. With `Flavored` you don't need to cast unflavored types: * ```ts type Email = Flavored<string, 'Email'>; const email1: Email = 'foo'; // ok type Address = Flavored<string, 'Address'>; const email2: Email = 'foo' as Address; // error! ``` * `Branded` requires casting: * ```ts type PhoneNumber = Branded<string, 'PhoneNumber'>; const phone1: PhoneNumber = 'foo'; // error! const phone2: PhoneNumber = 'foo' as PhoneNumber; // ok ``` * See also Zod's `.brand` schema helper:

declarations.name: format_url
declarations.docComment:
Formats a URL by removing 'https://', 'www.', and trailing slashes.
Notably it does not remove 'http://', so the user can see that it's insecure.

props.name: content
props.description: The source code to syntax highlight.

props.name: grammar
props.description:
Optional custom `SyntaxGrammar` object for syntax tokenization.

**When to use:**
- To provide a custom language definition not registered in `syntax_styler.langs`
- To use a modified/extended version of an existing grammar
- For one-off grammar variations without registering globally

**Behavior:**
- When provided, this grammar is used for tokenization instead of looking up via `lang`
- Enables highlighting even if `lang` is not in the registry (useful for custom languages)
- The `lang` parameter is still used for metadata (data-lang attribute)
- When undefined, the grammar is automatically looked up via `syntax_styler.get_lang(lang)`

props.name: inline
props.description:
Whether to render as inline code or block code.
Controls display via CSS classes.

props.name: wrap
props.description:
Whether to wrap long lines in block code.
Sets `white-space: pre-wrap` instead of `white-space: pre`.

**Behavior:**
- Wraps at whitespace (spaces, newlines)
- Long tokens without spaces (URLs, hashes) will still scroll horizontally
- Default `false` provides traditional code block behavior

Only affects block code (ignored for inline mode).

props.name: nomargin
props.description:
Whether to disable the default margin-bottom on block code.
Block code has `margin-bottom: var(--space_lg)` by default when not `:last-child`.

props.name: syntax_styler
props.description:
Custom `SyntaxStyler` instance to use for highlighting.
Allows using a different styler with custom grammars or configuration.

props.name: content

props.name: mode
props.description:
Highlighting mode for this component.

**Options:**
- `'auto'` - Uses CSS Custom Highlight API if supported, falls back to HTML mode
- `'ranges'` - Forces CSS Custom Highlight API (requires browser support)
- `'html'` - Forces HTML generation with CSS classes

**Note:** CSS Custom Highlight API has limitations and limited browser support.
Requires importing `theme_highlight.css` instead of `theme.css`.

- When undefined, the grammar is automatically looked up via `syntax_styler.get_lang(lang)`

members.name: add_lang
members.typeSignature: (id: string, grammar: SyntaxGrammarRaw, aliases?: string[] | undefined): void

members.name: stylize
members.docComment:
Generates HTML with syntax highlighting from source code.

**Process:**
1. Runs `before_tokenize` hook
2. Tokenizes code using the provided or looked-up grammar
3. Runs `after_tokenize` hook
4. Runs `wrap` hook on each token
5. Converts tokens to HTML with CSS classes

**Parameter Relationship:**
- `lang` is ALWAYS required for hook context and identification
- `grammar` is optional; when undefined, automatically looks up via `this.get_lang(lang)`
- When both are provided, `grammar` is used for tokenization, `lang` for metadata

**Use cases:**
- Standard usage: `stylize(code, 'ts')` - uses registered TypeScript grammar
- Custom grammar: `stylize(code, 'ts', customGrammar)` - uses custom grammar but keeps 'ts' label
- Extended grammar: `stylize(code, 'custom', this.extend_grammar('ts', extension))` - new language variant
members.typeSignature: (text: string, lang: string, grammar?: SyntaxGrammar | undefined): string

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: the source code to syntax highlight

parameters.name: lang
parameters.description:
language identifier (e.g., 'ts', 'css', 'html'), used for:
- grammar lookup when `grammar` is undefined
- hook context (`lang` field passed to hooks)
- language identification in output

parameters.name: grammar
parameters.description:
optional custom `SyntaxGrammar` object; when undefined, automatically
looks up the grammar via `this.get_lang(lang)`; provide this to use a custom
or modified grammar instead of the registered one

members.returnDescription: HTML string with syntax highlighting using CSS classes (`.token_*`)

members.name: grammar_insert_before
members.typeSignature: (inside: string, before: string, insert: SyntaxGrammarRaw, root?: Record<string, any>): SyntaxGrammar

parameters.name: inside
parameters.description:
the property of `root` (e.g. a language id in `syntax_styler.langs`) that contains the
object to be modified

parameters.name: insert
parameters.description: an object containing the key-value pairs to be inserted

parameters.name: root
parameters.description:
the object containing `inside`, i.e. the object that contains the
object to be modified; defaults to `syntax_styler.langs`

members.returnDescription: the new `SyntaxGrammar` object

members.name: stringify_token
members.docComment:
Converts the given token or token stream to an HTML representation.

Runs the `wrap` hook on each `SyntaxToken`.
members.typeSignature: (o: string | SyntaxTokenStream | SyntaxToken, lang: string): string

parameters.name: o
parameters.description: the token or `SyntaxTokenStream` to be converted

members.returnDescription: HTML representation of the token or token stream

members.name: extend_grammar
members.docComment:
Creates a deep copy of the language with the given id and appends the given tokens.

If a token in `extension` also appears in the copied language, then the existing token in the copied language
will be overwritten at its original position.

## Best practices

Since the position of overwriting tokens (token in `extension` that overwrite tokens in the copied language)
doesn't matter, they can technically be in any order. However, this can be confusing to others that trying to
understand the language definition because, normally, the order of tokens matters in the grammars.

Therefore, it is encouraged to order overwriting tokens according to the positions of the overwritten tokens.
Furthermore, all non-overwriting tokens should be placed after the overwriting ones.

parameters.name: base_id
parameters.description: the id of the language to extend, must be a key in `syntax_styler.langs`

members.returnDescription: the new `SyntaxGrammar`

declarations.name: SyntaxGrammarTokenRaw
declarations.docComment:
The expansion of a simple `RegExp` literal to support additional properties.

The `inside` grammar will be used to tokenize the text value of each token of this kind.

This can be used to make nested and even recursive language definitions.

Note: This can cause infinite recursion. Be careful when you embed different languages or even the same language into
each another.

Note: Grammar authors can use optional properties, but they will be normalized
to required properties at registration time for optimal performance.

members.name: pattern
members.docComment: The regular expression of the token.

members.name: lookbehind
members.docComment:
If `true`, then the first capturing group of `pattern` will (effectively)
behave as a lookbehind group meaning that the captured text will not be part of the matched text of the new token.

members.name: greedy
members.docComment: Whether the token is greedy.

members.name: alias
members.docComment: An optional alias or list of aliases.

members.name: inside
members.docComment: The nested `SyntaxGrammarRaw` of this token.

declarations.name: SyntaxGrammarToken
declarations.docComment:
Grammar token with all properties required.
This is the normalized representation of `SyntaxGrammarTokenRaw` used at runtime.

declarations.name: SyntaxGrammar
declarations.docComment:
A grammar after normalization.
All values are arrays of normalized `SyntaxGrammarToken` with consistent shapes.

declarations.name: syntax_styler_global
declarations.docComment: Pre-configured `SyntaxStyler` instance with all built-in grammars registered.

members.name: type
members.docComment:
The type of the token.

This is usually the key of a pattern in a `SyntaxGrammar`.

members.name: content
members.docComment:
The strings or tokens contained by this token.

This will be a `SyntaxTokenStream` if the pattern matched also defined an `inside` grammar.

members.name: alias
members.docComment:
The alias(es) of the token.
Always an array, even if empty or single value.

members.name: constructor
members.typeSignature: (type: string, content: string | SyntaxTokenStream, alias: string | string[] | undefined, matched_str?: string): SyntaxToken

declarations.name: SyntaxTokenStream
declarations.docComment:
A token stream is an array of strings and `SyntaxToken` objects.

`SyntaxTokenStream` values have to fulfill a few properties that are assumed by most functions
(mostly internal ones) that process them.

1. No adjacent strings.
2. No empty strings.

   The only exception here is the token stream that only contains the empty string and nothing else.

declarations.name: tokenize_syntax
declarations.docComment:
Accepts a string of text as input and the language definitions to use,
and returns an array with the tokenized code.

When the language definition includes nested tokens, the function is called recursively on each of these tokens.

This method could be useful in other contexts as well, as a very crude parser.

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: a string with the code to be styled

parameters.name: grammar
parameters.description:
a `SyntaxGrammar` object containing the tokens to use.
Usually a language definition like `syntax_styler.get_lang('markup')`.

declarations.returnDescription: a `SyntaxTokenStream` array of strings and tokens

declarations.name: parse_changeset_content
declarations.docComment:
Parses changeset content string from markdown format.

Pure function for testability - no file I/O, just string parsing.
Extracts package names, bump types, and summary from YAML frontmatter format.
Returns null if format is invalid or no packages found.

Expected format:
```
---
"package-name": patch
"@scope/package": minor
---

Summary of changes
```

parameters.name: filename

declarations.name: determine_bump_from_changesets
declarations.docComment:
Determines the bump type for a package from its changesets.

When multiple changesets exist for the same package, returns the highest
bump type (major > minor > patch) to ensure the most significant change
is reflected in the version bump.
declarations.returnDescription: the highest bump type, or null if package has no changesets

declarations.name: has_changesets
declarations.docComment:
Checks if a repo has any changeset files (excluding README.md).

Used by preflight checks and publishing workflow to determine which packages
need to be published. Returns false if `.changeset` directory doesn't exist
or contains only `README.md`.
declarations.returnDescription: true if repo has unpublished changesets

declarations.name: predict_next_version
declarations.docComment:
Predicts the next version by analyzing all changesets in a repo.

Reads all changesets, determines the highest bump type for the package,
and calculates the next version. Returns null if no changesets found.

Critical for dry-run mode accuracy - allows simulating publishes without
actually running `gro publish` which consumes changesets.
declarations.typeSignature: (repo: LocalRepo, log?: Logger | undefined): Promise<{ version: string; bump_type: BumpType; } | null>
declarations.returnType: Promise<{ version: string; bump_type: BumpType; } | null>
declarations.returnDescription: predicted version and bump type, or null if no changesets

declarations.name: DEPENDENCY_TYPE
declarations.typeSignature: { readonly PROD: "prod"; readonly PEER: "peer"; readonly DEV: "dev"; }

members.name: nodes
topological_sort
members.docComment:
Computes topological sort order for dependency graph.

Delegates to `@fuzdev/fuz_util/sort.js` for the sorting algorithm.
Throws if cycles detected.

throws.description: circular dependencies detected in included dependency types

parameters.name: exclude_dev
parameters.description:
if true, excludes dev dependencies to break cycles
Publishing uses `exclude_dev`=true to handle circular dev deps.

members.returnDescription: array of package names in dependency order (dependencies before dependents)

members.name: detect_cycles_by_type
members.docComment:
Detects circular dependencies, categorized by severity.

Production/peer cycles prevent publishing (impossible to order packages).
Dev cycles are normal (test utils, shared configs) and safely ignored.

Uses DFS traversal with recursion stack to identify back edges.
Deduplicates cycles using sorted cycle keys.
members.returnDescription: object with `production_cycles` (errors) and `dev_cycles` (info)

declarations.name: DependencyGraphBuilder
declarations.docComment: Builder for creating and analyzing dependency graphs.

members.name: build_from_repos
members.docComment:
Constructs dependency graph from local repos.

Two-pass algorithm: first creates nodes, then builds edges (dependents).
Prioritizes prod/peer deps over dev deps when same package appears in
multiple dependency types (stronger constraint wins).
members.returnDescription: fully initialized dependency graph with all nodes and edges

members.name: compute_publishing_order
members.docComment:
Computes publishing order using topological sort with dev deps excluded.

Excludes dev dependencies to break circular dev dependency cycles while
preserving production/peer dependency ordering. This allows patterns like
shared test utilities that depend on each other for development.

throws.description: production/peer cycles detected (cannot be resolved by exclusion)

members.returnDescription: package names in safe publishing order (dependencies before dependents)

members.name: analyze
members.typeSignature: (graph: DependencyGraph): { production_cycles: string[][]; dev_cycles: string[][]; wildcard_deps: { pkg: string; dep: string; version: string; }[]; missing_peers: { pkg: string; dep: string; }[]; }
members.returnType: { production_cycles: string[][]; dev_cycles: string[][]; wildcard_deps: { pkg: string; dep: string; version: string; }[]; missing_peers: { pkg: string; dep: string; }[]; }

modules.moduleComment:
Dependency graph data structure and algorithms for multi-repo publishing.

Provides `DependencyGraph` class with topological sort (via `@fuzdev/fuz_util/sort.js`)
and cycle detection by dependency type.
For validation workflow and publishing order computation, see `graph_validation.ts`.

declarations.name: update_package_json
declarations.docComment:
Updates `package.json` dependencies and creates changeset if needed.

Workflow:
1. Updates all dependency types (dependencies, devDependencies, peerDependencies)
2. Writes updated `package.json` with tabs formatting
3. Creates auto-changeset if `published_versions` provided (for transitive updates)
4. Commits both `package.json` and changeset with standard message

Uses version strategy to determine prefix (exact, caret, tilde) while preserving
existing prefixes when possible.
declarations.typeSignature: (repo: LocalRepo, updates: Map<string, string>, options?: UpdatePackageJsonOptions): Promise<void>

throws.description: file operations or git operations fail

declarations.name: update_all_repos
declarations.typeSignature: (repos: LocalRepo[], published: Map<string, string>, options?: UpdateAllReposOptions): Promise<{ updated: number; failed: { repo: string; error: Error; }[]; }>
declarations.returnType: Promise<{ updated: number; failed: { repo: string; error: Error; }[]; }>

declarations.name: find_updates_needed
declarations.typeSignature: (repo: LocalRepo, published: Map<string, string>): Map<string, { current: string; new: string; type: "dependencies" | "devDependencies" | "peerDependencies"; }>
declarations.returnType: Map<string, { current: string; new: string; type: "dependencies" | "devDependencies" | "peerDependencies"; }>

declarations.name: fetch_repo_data
declarations.docComment:
Fetches GitHub metadata (CI status, PRs) for all repos.

Fetches sequentially with delay between requests to respect GitHub API rate limits.
Uses `await_in_loop` intentionally to avoid parallel requests overwhelming the API.

Error handling: Logs fetch failures but continues processing remaining repos.
Repos with failed fetches will have `null` for `check_runs` or `pull_requests`.
declarations.typeSignature: (resolved_repos: LocalRepo[], token?: string | undefined, cache?: Map<string, { key: string; url: string; params: any; value: any; etag: string | null; last_modified: string | null; }> | undefined, log?: Logger | undefined, delay?: number, github_api_version?: string | undefined): Promise<...>

parameters.name: cache
parameters.type: Map<string, { key: string; url: string; params: any; value: any; etag: string | null; last_modified: string | null; }> | undefined
parameters.description: optional cache from `fuz_util`'s `fetch.js` for response memoization

declarations.returnDescription: array of `Repo` objects with GitHub metadata attached

declarations.name: create_fs_fetch_value_cache
declarations.docComment:
Creates file-system backed cache for `fuz_util`'s `fetch.js` API responses.

Cache invalidation strategy: If cache file can't be read or parsed, entire
cache is cleared (delete file) and starts fresh. This handles format changes.

Uses `structuredClone` to track changes - only writes to disk if data modified.
Formatted with Prettier before writing for version control friendliness.
declarations.returnDescription: cache object with Map-based data and `save()` method

'https://github.com/fuzdev/fuz_ui'

examples:

declarations.name: normalize_gitops_config
declarations.docComment:
Transforms a `RawGitopsConfig` to the more strict `GitopsConfig`.
This allows users to provide a more relaxed config.

declarations.name: validate_gitops_config_module
declarations.typeSignature: (config_module: any, config_path: string): asserts config_module is GitopsConfigModule

modules.moduleComment:
Configuration types and normalization for gitops multi-repo management.

Two-phase configuration system:
- `RawGitopsConfig` - User-friendly format with optional fields and flexible types
- `GitopsConfig` - Internal format with required fields and strict types

This allows users to provide minimal configs (e.g., just URLs as strings) while
the system works with normalized configs internally for type safety.

declarations.name: GITOPS_MAX_ITERATIONS_DEFAULT
declarations.docComment:
Maximum number of iterations for fixed-point iteration during publishing.
Used in both plan generation and actual publishing to resolve transitive dependency cascades.

In practice, most repos converge in 2-3 iterations.
Deep dependency chains may require more iterations.

declarations.name: GITOPS_CONFIG_PATH_DEFAULT
declarations.docComment: Default path to the gitops configuration file.

declarations.name: GITOPS_CONCURRENCY_DEFAULT
declarations.docComment: Default number of repos to process concurrently during parallel operations.

declarations.name: GITOPS_NPM_WAIT_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT
declarations.docComment:
Default timeout in milliseconds for waiting on NPM package propagation (10 minutes).
NPM's CDN uses eventual consistency, so published packages may not be immediately available.

modules.moduleComment:
Shared constants for gitops tasks and operations.

Naming convention: GITOPS_{NAME}_DEFAULT for user-facing defaults.

members.name: sync
members.docComment:
Sync each repo's working tree to its configured branch before loading
(switch branch, pull, install). When `false`, repos load exactly as they
sit on disk — the safe default for read-only diagnostics. Defaults to `true`.

members.name: allow_dirty
members.docComment: When syncing, tolerate uncommitted changes instead of throwing. Defaults to `false`.

declarations.name: get_gitops_ready
declarations.docComment:
Central initialization function for all gitops tasks.

Initialization sequence:
1. Loads and normalizes config from `gitops.config.ts`
2. Resolves local repo paths (creates missing with `--download`)
3. If `sync`, switches branches and pulls latest changes (in parallel by default)
4. If `sync`, auto-installs deps if `package.json` changed during pull

With `sync: false` (the default for read-only diagnostics), steps 3-4 are
skipped and repos are loaded exactly as checked out — no branch switch, pull,
install, or clean-workspace check.

Priority for path resolution:
- `dir` argument (explicit override)
- Config `repos_dir` setting
- `DEFAULT_REPOS_DIR` constant
declarations.typeSignature: (options: GetGitopsReadyOptions): Promise<{ config_path: string; repos_dir: string; gitops_config: GitopsConfig; local_repos: LocalRepo[]; }>

throws.description: config loading or repo resolution fails

propertyDescriptions.git_ops: for testing (defaults to real git operations)
propertyDescriptions.npm_ops: for testing (defaults to real npm operations)
propertyDescriptions.parallel: whether to load repos in parallel (default: true)
propertyDescriptions.sync: sync working trees before loading (default: true)
propertyDescriptions.allow_dirty: when syncing, tolerate uncommitted changes (default: false)

declarations.returnType: Promise<{ config_path: string; repos_dir: string; gitops_config: GitopsConfig; local_repos: LocalRepo[]; }>
declarations.returnDescription: initialized config and fully loaded repos ready for operations

modules.moduleComment:
Shared initialization logic for all gitops tasks.

Provides `get_gitops_ready()` which orchestrates:
- Config loading and normalization
- Repo resolution (local path discovery)
- Branch switching and syncing
- Dependency installation

Used by: `gitops_sync.task.ts`, `gitops_analyze.task.ts`, `gitops_plan.task.ts`,
`gitops_publish.task.ts`, and `gitops_validate.task.ts`.

Accepts `git_ops` and `npm_ops` parameters to support testing via operations pattern
(see `operations.ts` for dependency injection details).

declarations.name: validate_dependency_graph
declarations.docComment:
Shared utility for building dependency graph, detecting cycles, and computing publishing order.
This centralizes logic that was duplicated across `multi_repo_publisher`, `publishing_plan`, and `gitops_analyze`.
declarations.typeSignature: (repos: LocalRepo[], options?: { log?: Logger | undefined; throw_on_prod_cycles?: boolean | undefined; log_cycles?: boolean | undefined; log_order?: boolean | undefined; }): GraphValidationResult

throws.description: production cycles detected and `throw_on_prod_cycles` is true

propertyDescriptions.log_cycles: whether to log cycle information (default: true)
propertyDescriptions.log_order: whether to log publishing order (default: true)

declarations.returnDescription: graph validation result with graph, publishing order, and detected cycles
